title: Right to repair - an emerging consumer law debate in India
p1:
What if buying a product does not truly mean owning it?

A broken phone, laptop, or appliance often leaves consumers with only one choice: visit an authorised service centre or replace the product completely.
p2:
Many people assume manufacturers have complete control over repairs. But a growing consumer debate in India is challenging this idea: should buyers have the freedom to repair the products they own?

The 'right to repair' movement argues that consumers should have access to spare parts, repair manuals, and affordable repair options instead of depending only on the manufacturer.
p3:
This debate is not just about fixing gadgets. It affects consumer choice, repair businesses, electronic waste, and the cost of ownership. A product that can be repaired easily may last longer, saving money and reducing waste.

India has started addressing this issue through discussions around consumer rights and sustainable practices. The bigger question is finding a balance between consumer freedom, safety standards, and protection of innovation.
conclusion: Should consumers have a legal right to repair every product they buy, or should manufacturers decide how their products are serviced?
